actors: Player - participant who solves challenges and interacts via ticketing
Team - group of Players that share challenge instances
Organizer - CTF event manager who creates and configures challenges and oversees platform
Developer (DEV) - technical staff who receive tickets and maintain challenge instances
Admin - system administrator with full RBAC rights to manage users, roles, and platform settings
Cloud Provider - external service (e.g., AWS, GCP) that hosts on‑demand challenge instances
Ticketing Service - external or built‑in chat system for communication between Players, Organizers, and Developers
Background Worker - automated service that provisions and tears down challenge containers
Monitoring Service - external observability tool (e.g., Datadog, CloudWatch)
features: User registration with email/password and optional OAuth providers
User profile management (edit details, change password, view activity)
RBAC enforcement per functionality (Admin, Organizer, Developer, Player, Team)
Create new CTF challenge with description, flags, scoring, and difficulty metadata
Configure challenge cloud hosting settings (instance type, region, resource limits)
On‑demand provisioning of isolated web‑based challenge instance per Team when challenge is started
Automatic lifecycle management of challenge instances (start, stop, timeout, destroy)
Update and version control of challenge assets and deployment scripts
Global platform settings (registration policy, competition schedule, scoring rules)
Ticketing system with threaded chat between Players, Organizers, and Developers
Ticket creation, assignment, status tracking (open, in‑progress, resolved, closed)
Real‑time notifications for ticket updates and challenge instance status changes
Audit log of all RBAC‑protected actions and ticket interactions
Integration with paid CDN and load balancer for low‑latency challenge delivery
Use of managed relational database service for user, team, and challenge data
Use of managed cache service for session and instance state
Automated backups of database and challenge instance snapshots
Reporting dashboard for Organizers (team rankings, challenge solve rates, ticket metrics)
ambiguities: Exact granularity of RBAC permissions (e.g., can a Developer edit challenge settings or only respond to tickets?)
Whether challenge instances must persist state across Team sessions or be stateless per start
How “fast and lag free” is quantified and what latency targets are required for different regions
Preferred ticketing workflow (e.g., escalation paths, SLA response times)
Whether external cloud provider is pre‑selected or configurable per Organizer
How challenge versioning and rollback should be handled
If there is a need for real‑time scoreboard updates and how they are delivered
Whether multi‑language support is required for UI and challenge content
Details on backup retention period and disaster‑recovery RPO/RTO
Scope of audit logging (what actions, how long retained)
assumptions: The platform will use email/password authentication with optional OAuth (Google, GitHub) as initial login method
User data and challenge metadata will be stored in a managed PostgreSQL instance
Challenge instances will run in Docker containers orchestrated by a managed Kubernetes service (e.g., EKS, GKE)
Paid cloud services (compute, storage, CDN, load balancer) will be provisioned to meet low‑latency requirements
RBAC will be role‑based with hierarchical permissions defined by Admins and Organizers
Teams are limited to a maximum of 5 Players unless overridden by an Organizer
Challenge instances are isolated per Team and have a configurable maximum runtime (default 2 hours)
Ticketing will be implemented using a paid SaaS solution (e.g., Zendesk) or a hosted chat microservice
All communications will be served over HTTPS and use TLS 1.2+
The system expects up to 10 000 concurrent Players during peak CTF events
Performance SLA: challenge page load < 200 ms for users in supported regions
Logging and monitoring will be handled by a paid observability platform (e.g., Datadog)
Data privacy compliance will follow GDPR for EU participants and CCPA for US participants
missing_requirements: Security: password strength policy, MFA, rate limiting, protection against XSS/CSRF, secure storage of flags, secret management for cloud credentials
Performance: response time targets for registration, challenge launch, ticketing, and scoreboard updates
Scalability: auto‑scaling of web tier, challenge instance pool, database read replicas, CDN edge caching
Monitoring & Observability: health checks, alerting thresholds, request tracing, dashboarding for latency and error rates
Logging: structured logs for all services, retention policy, log aggregation
Backup & Recovery: automated daily backups, point‑in‑time restore, disaster recovery testing
Compliance: GDPR/CCPA data handling, consent management, data export/delete mechanisms
Accessibility: WCAG 2.1 AA compliance for UI components
Internationalization: support for multiple languages and locale‑specific date/number formats
Data Privacy: encryption at rest and in transit, data minimization, retention schedules
Error Handling: user‑friendly error pages, retry mechanisms, graceful degradation during service outages

modules.name: AuthService
modules.description: Handles user authentication, token issuance and revocation
responsibilities: Issue JWT access and refresh tokens

modules.name: UserService
modules.description: Manages user profiles and account lifecycle

modules.name: RBACService
modules.description: Enforces role‑based access control across the platform

modules.name: ChallengeService
modules.description: CRUD operations for CTF challenges and metadata

modules.name: InstanceService
modules.description: Provisions on‑demand challenge instances per team
responsibilities: Start cloud instance for a challenge

modules.name: SettingsService
modules.description: Stores and serves per‑challenge cloud hosting settings

modules.name: TicketService
modules.description: Ticketing system for communication between players, organizers and devs

modules.name: NotificationService
modules.description: Sends real‑time notifications and email alerts

modules.name: APIGateway
modules.description: Entry point routing external requests to internal services

modules.name: Frontend
modules.description: Web UI built for participants and organizers

database_entities.name: user_roles
database_entities.description: Many‑to‑many link between users and roles

database_entities.name: challenge_instances
database_entities.description: Running instances of a challenge for a team

database_entities.name: tickets
database_entities.description: Support tickets linking players, organizers and devs

database_entities.name: audit_logs
database_entities.description: Immutable audit trail of critical actions

mermaid_code:
flowchart LR
    classDef actor fill:#fff7ed,stroke:#c2410c,color:#1f2937
    classDef app fill:#ecfeff,stroke:#0891b2,color:#1f2937
    classDef service fill:#eef2ff,stroke:#4f46e5,color:#1f2937
    classDef data fill:#f0fdf4,stroke:#15803d,color:#1f2937
    classDef external fill:#fdf2f8,stroke:#be185d,color:#1f2937
    subgraph Channels
    N1_Frontend["Frontend"]
    class N1_Frontend app
    end
    subgraph Backend Services
    N2_Authservice["AuthService"]
    class N2_Authservice service
    N3_Userservice["UserService"]
    class N3_Userservice service
    N4_Rbacservice["RBACService"]
    class N4_Rbacservice service
    N5_Challengeservice["ChallengeService"]
    class N5_Challengeservice service
    N6_Instanceservice["InstanceService"]
    class N6_Instanceservice service
    N7_Settingsservice["SettingsService"]
    class N7_Settingsservice service
    N8_Ticketservice["TicketService"]
    class N8_Ticketservice service
    N9_Notificationservice["NotificationService"]
    class N9_Notificationservice service
    N10_Apigateway["APIGateway"]
    class N10_Apigateway service
    end
    subgraph Data Stores
    N11_Users[("users")]
    class N11_Users data
    N12_Roles[("roles")]
    class N12_Roles data
    N13_UserRoles[("user_roles")]
    class N13_UserRoles data
    N14_Teams[("teams")]
    class N14_Teams data
    N15_TeamMembers[("team_members")]
    class N15_TeamMembers data
    N16_Challenges[("challenges")]
    class N16_Challenges data
    N17_ChallengeSettings[("challenge_settings")]
    class N17_ChallengeSettings data
    N18_ChallengeInstances[("challenge_instances")]
    class N18_ChallengeInstances data
    N19_Tickets[("tickets")]
    class N19_Tickets data
    N20_TicketMessages[("ticket_messages")]
    class N20_TicketMessages data
    N21_AuditLogs[("audit_logs")]
    class N21_AuditLogs data
    end
    N22_User(["User"])
    N22_User --> N2_Authservice
    N2_Authservice --> N3_Userservice
    N3_Userservice --> N4_Rbacservice
    N4_Rbacservice --> N5_Challengeservice
    N5_Challengeservice --> N6_Instanceservice
    N6_Instanceservice --> N7_Settingsservice
    N7_Settingsservice --> N8_Ticketservice
    N8_Ticketservice --> N9_Notificationservice
    N9_Notificationservice --> N10_Apigateway
    N10_Apigateway --> N1_Frontend
    N1_Frontend --> N11_Users
    N1_Frontend --> N12_Roles
    N1_Frontend --> N13_UserRoles
    N1_Frontend --> N14_Teams
    N1_Frontend --> N15_TeamMembers
    N1_Frontend --> N16_Challenges
    N1_Frontend --> N17_ChallengeSettings
    N1_Frontend --> N18_ChallengeInstances
    N1_Frontend --> N19_Tickets
    N1_Frontend --> N20_TicketMessages
    N1_Frontend --> N21_AuditLogs
